clients.name: Sunway Group

scenarioImpacts.base: Brent at $104.73/bbl inflates construction fuel and cement-kiln energy costs ~22-28% vs prior quarter. MYR at 3.9280 provides partial offset vs USD-denominated material imports; Malaysia is a net oil exporter so MYR is relatively resilient. Property development pipeline faces margin compression; infrastructure segment partially insulated by fixed-price government contracts.
scenarioImpacts.stress: Oil toward $120/bbl raises Sunway's construction input cost base by est. 30-35%. Cement and steel surcharges compound margin pressure. MYR toward 4.10 vs USD would increase unhedged USD material exposure materially; revolving credit facility drawings likely to increase. Dubai Airport attack and Gulf hub risk adds supply-chain delays.
scenarioImpacts.tail: Brent at $150+ triggers cement and fuel surcharges across all project sites; est. MYR 380-450M incremental cost on in-flight portfolio. Property demand destruction from energy inflation; presales slowdown accelerates refinancing pressure on development-linked SPV bonds.

talkingPoints: Brent at $104.73/bbl is tracking +61% vs Q4 2025 average — Sunway's construction fuel and diesel procurement should be fully hedged for H2 2026; current unhedged exposure on active site count represents est. MYR 55-75M annualised risk per $10/bbl move.

MYR at 3.9280 is relatively resilient vs oil-importer ASEAN peers (THB weakened 2.2% to 32.47 this week) — use MYR strength vs THB and PHP to front-load any cross-border procurement before Malaysia-specific oil revenue premium narrows.

Property development SPV bonds maturing 2027-2028 should be pre-financed now while iTraxx Asia IG at ~163bp is still executable; ASEAN HY at ~510bp confirms the window for investment-grade issuers is closing — move immediately to term out SPV debt before spread widening extends further.

clients.name: IJM Corporation

scenarioImpacts.base: Plantation segment benefits from CPO prices tracking Brent at $104.73 (high correlation); infrastructure segment faces significantly higher diesel and bitumen costs. Shipping costs for palm oil exports elevated as Gulf rerouting compresses regional shipping capacity. MYR at 3.9280 creates mixed FX impact — exports benefit, USD imports hurt.
scenarioImpacts.stress: Brent at $120/bbl translates to CPO prices tracking $1,350+/MT — plantation revenue improves but is offset by infrastructure cost inflation. Port and logistics operations face surging bunker costs. Dubai Airport attack adds Gulf transit risk to Penang and Johor port volumes. Net: marginally negative at group level given infrastructure-heavy earnings mix.
scenarioImpacts.tail: Extended Hormuz and Gulf hub closure redirects ASEAN-Middle East shipping; IJM's port concessions face volume decline. Plantation export logistics disrupted if Strait of Malacca re-routing congestion adds transit costs. Refinancing of MYR 1.2bn infrastructure sukuk warrants urgent review if credit spreads blow out beyond 200bp.

talkingPoints: CPO prices tracking crude oil upward (Brent at $104.73 → CPO correlation ~0.72); IJM's plantation segment is a meaningful earnings offset against oil shock — update earnings model to reflect revised CPO trajectory above $1,200/MT for H1 2026.

Infrastructure contract pipeline: renegotiate fuel escalation provisions on active tenders before locking in sub-$90/bbl diesel assumptions in bid pricing — Brent at $104.73 makes any fixed-price fuel clause in a 2026 tender extremely risky.

MYR sukuk maturing 2026-2027: window to refinance at competitive rates is compressing as ASEAN HY is at ~510bp and iTraxx IG ~163bp; recommend mandate selection within 2 weeks while Malaysian sovereign spread still outperforms oil-importer peers.

clients.name: Wilmar International

scenarioImpacts.base: Palm oil/biodiesel pricing directly tracks Brent at $104.73 — CPO refining margins and biodiesel mandates (Indonesia B40, Malaysia B20) remain highly compelling. Bunker costs (~12% of Wilmar's cost base) reflect elevated freight environment. Dubai Airport attack adds logistical uncertainty for Gulf cargo legs. USD/SGD at 1.2782 broadly stable.
scenarioImpacts.stress: Oil at $120/bbl creates dual impact: biodiesel margin expansion (+USD 100-120/MT) partially offset by bunker cost surge. Wilmar's Middle East cargo legs face insurance void and port-access risk post-Dubai Airport attack. Supply chain re-routing around Hormuz adds 10-18 days. SGD at 1.28 broadly stable but demand for USD liquidity rises.
scenarioImpacts.tail: Hormuz and Gulf hub closure disrupts Wilmar's Middle East bulk trade; est. $250-320M incremental annual logistics cost at $150/bbl oil. Biodiesel revenue surge partially offsets. Revolving credit utilisation spikes; bank appetite for commodity trader credit under sanctions pressure tightens further.

talkingPoints: Brent at $104.73/bbl makes Indonesia's B40 biodiesel mandate highly compelling — Wilmar is the largest biodiesel refiner in ASEAN; model revenue uplift from mandatory blending at current crude economics shows $450-520M incremental EBITDA vs $60/bbl base case; update earnings model immediately.

Dubai Airport drone strike on Mar 16 adds Gulf hub logistics risk to Wilmar's Middle East cargo book — review all UAE-port-dependent shipment schedules and contingency-route arrangements; bunker hedging should now extend to Q1 2027 given sustained disruption horizon.

USD revolving credit facility: with iTraxx Asia IG at ~163bp and widening, pre-emptive draw-down of undrawn RCF tranches locks in current margin; reduces refinancing risk if commodity trade finance bank appetite tightens further under Iran secondary sanctions pressure.

clients.name: Hyflux

scenarioImpacts.base: Desalination operations are extremely energy-intensive — electricity costs represent 35-45% of total operating cost. Brent at $104.73/bbl drives Singapore grid power costs significantly higher vs Q4 2025. Legacy restructuring means Hyflux balance sheet remains highly leveraged; restructuring creditor repayment schedule linked to operational cashflows now under severe energy cost pressure.
scenarioImpacts.stress: Power cost at $120/bbl oil equivalent pushes desalination OPEX above contractual tariff recovery thresholds. Refinancing creditors become anxious; restructuring plan covenant compliance at risk. ASEAN HY spreads at ~510bp make any new capital raise prohibitively expensive — further restructuring negotiation is required.
scenarioImpacts.tail: Energy cost spiral under $150+ oil renders Hyflux's desalination economics non-viable without emergency tariff relief. Creditor acceleration risk materialises; Singapore government may need to intervene to ensure water supply continuity. Restructuring collapses into administration unless sovereign backstop is committed.

talkingPoints: Hyflux desalination OPEX is 38-42% electricity; with Singapore power prices tracking Brent at $104.73, the cost-recovery gap vs contracted tariff has widened to est. SGD 35-45M annually — urgent need to renegotiate power purchase agreements or escalation clauses in government supply contracts.

Restructuring plan repayment milestones fall due H2 2026 — ASEAN HY spreads at ~510bp effectively close the capital markets window; engage private-credit and DFI/multilateral bridge financing options now before energy cost deterioration worsens creditor confidence further.

Base scenario probability is 20% and declining; Brent at $104.73 and Gulf infrastructure attacks on Mar 16 (Dubai Airport, Fujairah) eliminate the near-term de-escalation path that was previously the only viable timing window for market normalisation and capital access.

clients.name: Sapura Energy

scenarioImpacts.base: Brent at $104.73/bbl is well above the $65-70 E&P capex trigger threshold for most upstream clients; new contract awards improving but execution lagging 2-3 quarters. Sapura's high leverage (net debt ~MYR 10bn) means widening ASEAN HY spreads (~510bp) directly impact refinancing cost. Shipping utilisation for marine vessels recovering modestly.
scenarioImpacts.stress: Oil at $120/bbl boosts E&P client confidence but capex cycle lag means Sapura revenue uplift is 6-9 months behind oil price. Existing debt refinancing under severe stress: ASEAN HY at 510bp+ makes bond issuance economically unfeasible. Dubai Airport attack adds Gulf project logistics risk for any Sapura upstream contracts in the region.
scenarioImpacts.tail: E&P capex freezes under $150 oil uncertainty (client hedging collapse) — paradoxically negative for oil services despite high prices. Sapura debt covenant breach risk materialises; government-linked creditor (Petronas, Maybank) intervention required. Potential restructuring or asset sale of marine fleet to service debt obligations.

talkingPoints: Brent at $104.73/bbl is well above Sapura's E&P client capex threshold — but order book recovery is lagging the oil price by 2-3 quarters; model 2026 revenue at $85/bbl equivalent to avoid overcounting near-term uplift in board presentations.

Sapura's MYR debt maturities 2026-2027 represent the critical refinancing risk: iTraxx Asia IG at ~163bp and ASEAN HY at ~510bp signals the unsecured window is effectively closed — pivot immediately to secured bank refinancing and bilateral lender negotiations before covenants are at risk.

Dubai Airport attack and Gulf infrastructure targeting on Mar 16 add new compliance and operational risk for any Sapura upstream contracts in UAE or adjacent Gulf geographies — conduct an immediate review of project insurance, force majeure provisions, and sanctions compliance for all Gulf-region contracts.

clients.name: PTTEP

scenarioImpacts.base: PTTEP is a net beneficiary of Brent at $104.73/bbl — upstream producer with production cost est. $28-32/bbl; operating margin expands materially. USD/THB at 32.47 (weakened 2.2% from Mar 13) reduces USD revenue on repatriation vs earlier this week. LNG and Thailand domestic gas supply obligations provide stable domestic revenue floor.
scenarioImpacts.stress: Oil at $120/bbl boosts PTTEP upstream EBITDA by est. USD 600-750M annually. However, Dubai Airport attack and Gulf hub targeting create sanctions compliance risk for any PTTEP Middle East JV operations. THB weakening toward 34-35 in a full stress scenario reduces repatriation value materially.
scenarioImpacts.tail: Extended Hormuz and Gulf hub closure raises PTTEP's shipping and logistics cost for LNG offtake; rerouting adds significant cost to Mozambique and Myanmar offshore logistics. THB depreciation toward 36-38 in a systemic tail scenario would reduce USD revenue translation. Sanctions scrutiny on Middle East upstream JVs warrants immediate compliance review.

talkingPoints: PTTEP is directly benefiting from Brent at $104.73 — upstream production cost at $28-32/bbl means current oil price generates operating margins of $73-77/bbl; accelerate hedging of remaining 2026 unhedged production at current levels before any coalition breakthrough or ceasefire retracement.

THB has weakened to 32.47 (from 31.76 on Mar 13, -2.2%) on Dubai Airport attack and Gulf escalation — the THB bond prefunding window has narrowed; Thailand 10Y at est. 1.97% is still executable but BOT's ability to ease is further constrained by oil-driven inflation; execute H2 2026 prefunding plan this week.

Dubai Airport drone strike on Mar 16 and continued Gulf hub targeting increase the likelihood of secondary sanctions compliance scrutiny on PTTEP's Middle East JV operations — conduct an immediate jurisdictional review of all Gulf-region exploration assets and JV partner relationships to ensure full OFAC and EU compliance.
